airports.name: I Gusti Ngurah Rai International
airports.note: Direct long-haul and regional flights; also the gateway airport for Nusa Penida and the fast-boat routes east

Bali.getting_in: Fly straight into Denpasar (DPS), which has nonstop links from most major Asian and Australian hubs plus Doha and Istanbul — no domestic leg needed. The airport sits between Kuta and Jimbaran, 30-45 min by road from Sanur and about 1.5 h from Ubud.

options.note: Fixed-price airport taxi counter; ~30-45 min to Sanur (~IDR 150,000-250,000), ~90 min to Ubud (~IDR 300,000-400,000)
Ride-hailing apps work from designated airport pickup zones at similar or slightly lower rates
Hotel and dive-area pickups bookable in advance; count on 2.5-3 h to Amed/Tulamben on the east coast

car.note: Driving is on the left and southern Bali traffic is dense with scooters; a car with driver runs about USD 40-50 for a full day, so most visitors skip self-driving. Confident riders rent scooters (~IDR 70,000-100,000/day) outside the congested south.

stay.why: Calm beachfront grid with the fast-boat harbour to Nusa Penida at its doorstep and an easy 30-45 min airport run
Quiet fishing villages on the northeast coast, right on the shore-entry dive coast and far from the southern traffic
Inland base among rice terraces and temples with the island's densest concentration of culture, crafts and restaurants
Slow northwest-coast village handy for the national-park islands and a night-and-day contrast to the busy south
The food, café and nightlife belt on the southwest surf beaches, best for the non-diving days at either end of a trip

todo.title: Kecak dance at Uluwatu Temple
todo.note: A clifftop temple 70 m above the surf where a 60-man chanting choir performs the Ramayana at sunset. Arrive an hour early for a seat on the west side of the amphitheatre.

todo.title: Mount Batur sunrise trek
todo.note: A 2-h pre-dawn climb to the 1,717 m crater rim for sunrise over Lake Batur and Mount Agung. Pickups run around 02:00-03:00 and you are back by mid-morning.

todo.title: Jatiluwih rice terraces
todo.note: 600 ha of UNESCO-listed terraces on the slopes of Mount Batukaru, laced with marked walking loops of 1.5-5 km. Far less crowded than the Instagram terraces near Ubud.

todo.title: Tirta Empul water temple
todo.note: A 10th-century spring temple where Balinese Hindus queue under 13 water spouts for ritual purification; visitors can join with a rented sarong. Combine with the nearby Gunung Kawi rock-cut shrines.

todo.title: Ubud Monkey Forest and old town
todo.note: A 12.5 ha sacred forest with three temples and about 1,200 long-tailed macaques, five minutes' walk from Ubud's palace and market. Keep bags zipped — the monkeys are practised thieves.

todo.title: Balinese cooking class
todo.note: Most classes start with a morning produce-market visit, then work through base gede spice paste, satay lilit and lawar in an open kitchen. A practical crash course in why Balinese food tastes nothing like Java's.

todo.title: Sekumpul waterfall trek
todo.note: A cluster of 80 m falls in the north-coast hills, reached by a steep 30-40 min descent and two river crossings. It is a 2.5 h drive from the south, so pair it with north-coast villages for a full day.

todo.title: Sidemen valley walk or cycle
todo.note: Rice-field lanes and weaving villages under Mount Agung in east Bali, still largely tour-bus-free. Handy on the way to or from the Amed dive coast.

todo.title: Beginner surf lesson on the southwest beaches
todo.note: The beach breaks from Kuta up to Canggu offer soft sandbar waves and board rental on the sand. A 2-h lesson typically gets first-timers standing.
todo.q: beginner surf lesson bali beach break

events.name: Nyepi (Day of Silence)
events.when: March (date follows the Saka lunar calendar; 19 March in 2026)
events.note: The whole island stops for 24 h — the airport closes, roads empty and guests must stay inside their hotel; the ogoh-ogoh demon parades the evening before are worth seeing

events.name: Galungan & Kuningan
events.when: Every 210 days on the pawukon cycle, so the month shifts each year (17 and 27 June in 2026)
events.note: Bamboo penjor poles arch over every village street and temples fill with processions; businesses close for the main days

events.name: Bali Arts Festival
events.when: Mid-June to mid-July, month-long
events.note: Daily gamelan, dance and craft performances at the Denpasar arts centre, most of them free

events.name: Bali Kite Festival
events.when: July, during the windy season (kites fly over Sanur through October)
events.note: Village teams launch traditional kites up to 10 m across from Padang Galak beach near Sanur

Bali.topside_summary: Bali layers volcanoes, rice terraces and a living Hindu temple culture onto a compact island where daily offerings still appear on every doorstep. The south is dense with surf beaches, restaurants and traffic; Ubud anchors the artistic interior; the east and north coasts stay rural and quiet. Food ranges from IDR 25,000 warung nasi campur to a serious modern dining scene, and ceremony processions will genuinely stop your car.

airports.name: I Gusti Ngurah Rai International
airports.note: Nearest airport — Nusa Penida has none; cross by fast boat from Sanur

Nusa Penida.getting_in: Fly to Denpasar (DPS), transfer 30-45 min to Sanur harbour, then take a fast boat to Toyapakeh or Sampalan — about 21 daily sailings between 06:30 and 17:00, 30-45 min crossing, IDR 150,000-250,000 each way. Slower and cheaper boats also run from Padang Bai and Kusamba on Bali's east coast.

options.note: Sanur to Toyapakeh/Sampalan, 30-45 min, ~USD 10-15 one way; book ahead in July-August and around Balinese ceremony days
On-island cars with driver are the standard way to tour: priced per vehicle for a full day with fuel included, slightly above Bali's usual USD 40-50 day-driver rate

car.note: With a driver, yes — there are no taxis or ride-hailing apps on the island. Roads to the headline viewpoints are steep, narrow and part-broken, so the IDR 70,000-100,000/day scooters are only for genuinely experienced riders.

stay.why: Beside the main fast-boat jetty and the closest base to the west-coast big-hitters like Kelingking and Broken Beach
The island's main strip of guesthouses, warungs and dive shops along the calm north shore road
A quieter western pocket with a swimmable sunset beach ten minutes from Toyapakeh

todo.title: Kelingking viewpoint
todo.note: The 200 m limestone headland shaped like a T-rex, with the island's most photographed clifftop panorama. The optional descent to the beach is a steep, exposed 40-60 min scramble — the view alone justifies the trip.

todo.title: Broken Beach and Angel's Billabong
todo.note: A collapsed sea cave forming a natural rock arch around a circular cove, next to a tide-carved natural infinity pool. Visit at low tide and stay out of the pool when swell is running.

todo.title: Diamond Beach and Atuh Beach
todo.note: Twin white-sand coves under limestone pinnacles on the east tip, reached by cliff-cut staircases. The east-coast drive from Sampalan takes about an hour each way.

todo.title: Peguyangan waterfall stairs
todo.note: A blue steel staircase bolted down a 200 m sea cliff to a spring-fed temple and bathing pools at the bottom. Sarongs required — it is an active pilgrimage site, not just a photo stop.

todo.title: Teletubbies Hills
todo.note: Rounded green karst hummocks across the island's dry southeast interior, best in the late-afternoon light. An easy add-on to the Diamond Beach run.

todo.title: Crystal Bay sunset
todo.note: The island's most swimmable beach, a palm-backed cove facing due west toward Nusa Ceningan. Warungs on the sand sell grilled corn and coconuts for the sunset hour.

todo.title: Tembeling forest and rock pools
todo.note: A jungle track drops to two spring-fed pools and a tiny cove on the wild south coast. The final stretch is only passable by ojek or on foot, which keeps the crowds off.

todo.title: Pura Dalem Ped temple
todo.note: One of Bali's most important pilgrimage temples, drawing boatloads of worshippers from the mainland on ceremony days. It sits right on the north-coast road, so a respectful 30-minute stop is easy.

events.name: Nyepi (Day of Silence)
events.when: March (Saka lunar calendar; 19 March in 2026)
events.note: Penida observes the silence like the rest of Bali province — all boats stop and guests stay in their accommodation for 24 h, with Denpasar airport also closed

events.name: Galungan & Kuningan pilgrimages
events.when: Every 210 days on the pawukon cycle, so the month shifts each year (June in 2026)
events.note: Pura Dalem Ped fills with pilgrims crossing from mainland Bali; fast boats sell out around the holy days

Nusa Penida.topside_summary: Nusa Penida is a raw limestone plateau 45 minutes off Bali that skipped the mainland's development curve: seaweed farms, cassava plots and cliff-edge temples, with 200 m sea cliffs on its south coast. Infrastructure is basic and roads rough, which is exactly the appeal — days are spent bouncing between viewpoints, stair-cut beaches and warung lunches. Evenings are quiet; this is an early-nights island.

airports.name: Lombok International (Zainuddin Abdul Madjid)
airports.note: Domestic hub plus short-haul international (Kuala Lumpur, Singapore); ~2 h drive from Bangsal harbour for the Gilis

airports.name: I Gusti Ngurah Rai International
airports.note: The usual long-haul gateway, connected to the Gilis by direct fast boats

Gili Islands & Lombok.getting_in: Two workable routes: fly long-haul into Bali (DPS) and take a direct fast boat from Padang Bai or Serangan to Gili Trawangan (1.5-2.5 h, ~USD 22-35), or fly into Lombok (LOP) — 30 min from Bali, or nonstop from Jakarta, Kuala Lumpur and Singapore — then drive about 2 h north to Bangsal harbour for the 20-45 min boat hop. For south-Lombok stays, Kuta Lombok is only 30 min from LOP.

options.note: Bali (Padang Bai) to Gili Trawangan, 1.5-2 h, ~USD 22-35 one way; several morning departures, seas roughest December-February
LOP airport to Bangsal harbour ~2 h, around USD 25-50 per car; to Kuta Lombok ~30 min
Public boats from Bangsal to the Gilis, 20-45 min, under IDR 50,000 — they leave when full, so allow slack

car.note: The Gilis ban all motor vehicles — you walk, cycle (~IDR 50,000/day), take a cidomo horse cart (IDR 100,000-150,000 a hop) or rent an e-bike (IDR 250,000-350,000/day). On Lombok itself, driving is on the left, traffic is far lighter than Bali, and a scooter or car with driver covers the waterfalls and south-coast beaches well.

stay.why: The largest island with the widest spread of restaurants, dive shops and nightlife, plus the most boat connections
Closest island to Lombok with a middle-ground pace — enough cafés to be easy, quiet enough to sleep
The smallest and quietest of the three, essentially a honeymoon-and-hammock island
South-coast surf town 30 min from the airport, the base for Mandalika, Sasak villages and the best beach road-trips

todo.title: Cycle the Gili Trawangan loop
todo.note: The flat 7 km coastal track circles the island in about an hour, passing salt flats and the quiet north beaches. Push the bike through the two soft-sand stretches on the west side.

todo.title: Sunset strip on Gili T's west coast
todo.note: The west-facing beach bars line up swings and beanbags for sunset behind Bali's Mount Agung across the strait. Come down from about 17:30 for the show.

todo.title: Gili Trawangan night market
todo.note: Evening stalls by the harbour grill fresh reef fish and serve nasi campur to shared tables for a few dollars a plate. Go early — the best fish sells out by 20:00.

todo.title: Island-hop to Gili Meno and Gili Air
todo.note: Public island-hopping boats link the three Gilis twice daily for pocket change. Meno's salt lake and empty east-coast sand make the laziest of day trips.

todo.title: Surf lesson at Selong Belanak
todo.note: A horseshoe bay near Kuta Lombok with a sandy-bottomed beginner wave and board rentals on the beach. One of the easiest places in Indonesia to catch a first green wave.

todo.title: Tiu Kelep and Sendang Gile waterfalls
todo.note: Two falls on Rinjani's northern foothills near Senaru, the second reached by a 45-min river-wading walk into a mossy amphitheatre. It pairs naturally with the drive along the scenic north coast from Bangsal.
todo.q: sendang gile tiu kelep waterfall senaru

todo.title: Sasak village visit (Sade or Ende)
todo.note: Traditional villages of bamboo-and-thatch lumbung houses where weaving is still done on backstrap looms. Ten minutes from Kuta Lombok; a local guide and a small donation are expected.

todo.title: Tetebatu rice terraces
todo.note: Rinjani's green southern slopes with terrace walks through tobacco and rice plots and a monkey-inhabited forest — Lombok's quieter answer to Ubud's countryside. About 1.5 h from Kuta Lombok.

todo.title: Rinjani crater rim trek
todo.note: Indonesia's second-highest volcano (3,726 m) with a crater-lake panorama from the 2,639 m Senaru rim; the standard trip is a guided 2-day/1-night camp. Only for a spare weather-window before flying — no diving after a high-altitude night.

events.name: Bau Nyale festival
events.when: February (date set by the tenth month of the Sasak lunar calendar)
events.note: Tens of thousands descend on the Kuta/Mandalika beaches at dawn to harvest sea worms tied to a princess legend — south-coast rooms sell out

events.name: Idul Fitri (Lebaran)
events.when: End of Ramadan; the Islamic calendar moves it ~11 days earlier each year (falls in March in 2026)
events.note: Lombok is majority Muslim — domestic flights, fast boats and hotels fill for about a week, and mainland restaurants keep daytime hours low-key during Ramadan itself

events.name: Indonesian motorcycle grand prix, Mandalika
events.when: A weekend in early October
events.note: The street circuit sits beside Kuta Lombok; accommodation across the south coast books out and prices spike for race week

events.name: Perang Topat
events.when: Late November or December, on the full moon of the seventh Sasak month
events.note: A good-natured 'rice-cake war' between Hindu and Muslim communities at Lingsar temple near Mataram — an easy half-day from Senggigi or the Gilis

Gili Islands & Lombok.topside_summary: The three Gilis are flat coral cays with no motor vehicles — days run on bicycles, beach naps and grilled fish, with Trawangan supplying the nightlife and Meno the silence. Lombok next door is a different register entirely: a Sasak, majority-Muslim island of surf bays, tobacco fields and the 3,726 m Rinjani volcano. Split a week between sandbar idleness and south-coast road trips and neither half feels like filler.

airports.name: Komodo International
airports.note: On Flores, the sole gateway town for the park; the airport is ~10-15 min from the harbour

Komodo National Park.getting_in: Fly to Labuan Bajo (LBJ) on Flores: about 1 h nonstop from Bali with 4-6 daily flights, or ~2.5 h nonstop from Jakarta. The airport is a 10-15 min drive from town and the harbour, from where all park boats — day trips and liveaboards — depart.

options.note: Fixed-rate airport cars into town, 10-15 min, roughly IDR 50,000-100,000; most hotels also offer free or cheap pickups
Boats board directly at Labuan Bajo harbour or the marina, 10 min from the airport — same-day connections from a morning Bali flight are routine

car.note: Labuan Bajo is a walkable one-road harbour town and the park itself is reached only by boat. Rent a scooter (~IDR 75,000/day, left-hand driving) for hillside cafés and nearby caves; hire a car with driver only for full-day inland Flores trips.

stay.why: Steps from the jetties for 05:30 park departures, with the town's restaurant row and trip offices on the same street
Guesthouses and hotels a short, steep ride up get harbour-sunset views and cooler air away from the street noise
Beach resorts 10-15 min north of town for a quieter base that still reaches the harbour easily

todo.title: Padar Island sunrise hike
todo.note: A 30-40 min stair climb to the ridge view over Padar's three differently-coloured bays, the park's signature panorama. Done as the first stop on a boat day leaving Labuan Bajo around 05:00-06:00.

todo.title: Ranger-guided dragon walk on Komodo or Rinca
todo.note: The only place on earth to see wild Komodo dragons — 2-3 m monitors that rangers track on short guided loops past deer and wild pigs. Rinca's boardwalk circuit is shorter; Komodo island's savannah walks feel wilder.

todo.title: Pink Beach (Pantai Merah)
todo.note: Crushed red organ-pipe coral tints the sand pink against turquoise shallows; it is a standard swim-and-lunch stop on park boat itineraries. Late morning gives the strongest colour.

todo.title: Flying foxes at Kalong Island
todo.note: At dusk thousands of fruit bats stream off a mangrove islet toward Flores in a river of wings that lasts half an hour. Overnight boats anchor here; day boats sometimes catch it on the way home.

todo.title: Wae Rebo village
todo.note: A ring of seven cone-roofed Manggarai clan houses at 1,100 m, reached by a 4-5 h drive and a 2-3 h forest hike; guests sleep on mats in a communal house. Usually run as an overnight from Labuan Bajo — the single best cultural trip on Flores.

todo.title: Cunca Wulang waterfall and canyon
todo.note: A slot canyon with jump-able pools and small falls about 1.5 h inland from town, plus a 30-40 min guided walk through candlenut and cacao gardens. The reliable pick for a surface-interval day.

todo.title: Rangko Cave swim
todo.note: A tidal cave near Boleng, 45 min drive plus a short boat hop, holding a clear turquoise saltwater pool lit by a shaft of midday sun. Go around low tide when the ledge for entry is exposed.

todo.title: Caci whip-duel performance at Melo village
todo.note: Manggarai men duel with rattan whips and buffalo-hide shields in a ritual display of courage, staged for visitors in a hill village 20 km from town. Book as a half-day trip with hotel pickup.

events.name: Penti harvest ceremony at Wae Rebo
events.when: November each year (exact days set by the village)
events.note: The Manggarai new-year thanksgiving with all-night ritual, gong music and caci — the village's few guest mats book out well ahead

events.name: Indonesian Independence Day
events.when: 17 August
events.note: Flag ceremonies, street games and packed domestic flights in the days around it — book LBJ seats early

events.name: Idul Fitri (Lebaran) travel surge
events.when: End of Ramadan; the Islamic calendar moves it ~11 days earlier each year (March in 2026)
events.note: A week-long national holiday when flights to Labuan Bajo and park boats fill with domestic travellers

Komodo National Park.topside_summary: The park is a cluster of savannah islands — dry, golden and dragon-patrolled — while the base town of Labuan Bajo is a fast-growing harbour strip on Flores with a sunset-facing restaurant row above the jetties. Topside days revolve around ridge hikes, ranger walks and boat-deck hours; inland Flores adds waterfalls, cacao gardens and the cone-roofed mountain village of Wae Rebo. Manggarai culture surfaces in caci whip duels and strong local coffee.

airports.name: Domine Eduard Osok
airports.note: Reached via Jakarta (nonstop ~4 h, mostly overnight departures) or Makassar; onward by ferry to Waisai on Waigeo

Raja Ampat.getting_in: Fly to Sorong (SOQ) in Southwest Papua — nonstop from Jakarta in about 4 h (typically red-eyes) or via Makassar — then take the ~2 h fast ferry from Sorong harbour to Waisai on Waigeo, which runs once or twice daily. Homestays and resorts collect guests by boat from Waisai jetty; budget for the marine-park permit (IDR 700,000) and visitor entry ticket (IDR 300,000) payable in Waisai or online.

options.note: Sorong airport to the ferry harbour, 15-20 min, around IDR 100,000
Sorong-Waisai fast ferry, ~2 h: economy IDR 110,000-137,000, VIP cabin IDR 250,000-262,000, plus a small boarding fee; arrive at least an hour early
Homestay or resort boat pickup from Waisai jetty, 20 min-2 h depending on the island, usually charged per boat and arranged when booking
Liveaboards board directly in Sorong harbour or the marina, skipping the ferry entirely

car.note: There is nothing to drive: beyond Waisai's few streets the archipelago has no road network, and every stay, village and viewpoint is reached by boat. Plan around fuel costs — boat petrol is the biggest line item after the room.

stay.why: The main homestay-and-resort belt in the Dampier Strait, minutes by boat from the region's busiest reefs and jetties
Quieter over-water homestays around Kabui Bay's karst, well placed for bird-of-paradise walks and mangrove paddling
The small capital with the ferry jetty, ATMs and permit offices — practical for first and last nights rather than the whole stay

todo.title: Piaynemo viewpoint and Star Lagoon
todo.note: A 320-step boardwalk climbs to the postcard view over cone karst islets in a turquoise lagoon, with the star-shaped Telaga Bintang lookout nearby. It is a 1.5-2 h boat run from the Dampier Strait, so it fills a day with lagoon stops en route.

todo.title: Red bird-of-paradise dawn walk
todo.note: Local guides in Saporkren or Sawinggrai lead pre-dawn forest walks to display trees where red birds-of-paradise dance at first light. Departure is around 04:30-05:00; back for breakfast.
todo.q: red bird of paradise watching waigeo

todo.title: Arborek village visit
todo.note: A tidy 200-person island village known for woven-palm handicrafts and a lively jetty, and an easy model of how tourism income works here. Combine with a sandbar or lagoon stop on the same boat.

todo.title: Paddle the Kabui Bay karst and mangroves
todo.note: Kayaks or dugouts thread between mushroom-shaped islets, hidden coves and the narrow 'passage' channel between Gam and Waigeo. Calm, shaded water — the best low-effort afternoon in the islands.

todo.title: Pasir Timbul sandbar
todo.note: A bright white sandbar that surfaces only around low tide in the middle of the strait. Boats time a swim-and-photo stop around the tide tables — ask your hosts which day works.

todo.title: Kali Biru blue river
todo.note: A spring-fed river in the Warsambin forest east of Waisai that runs an improbable electric blue and stays cold enough to gasp. Reached by boat up the bay plus a short walk; pay the village fee on arrival.

events.name: Raja Ampat Festival
events.when: Mid-to-late October, in Waisai
events.note: Several days of Papuan dance, boat races, crafts and seafood stalls on the Waisai waterfront — ferries and town rooms run full

events.name: Christmas and New Year
events.when: Late December to early January
events.note: The islands are largely Christian: villages hold week-long church celebrations, some homestays close for family time, and ferries fill — confirm ahead

Raja Ampat.topside_summary: Raja Ampat is an archipelago of jungle-covered karst islands off the Bird's Head of New Guinea, where life runs on boat schedules, rainwater tanks and generator hours. Villages are small, Christian and Papuan, meals are grilled fish and sago-based staples at the homestay table, and the loudest evening sound is rain on a thatch roof. It rewards travellers who want dawn birdsong and hammock time rather than restaurants — bring cash, patience and dry bags.

airports.name: Sharm El Sheikh International
airports.note: Direct scheduled and charter flights from most of Europe and the Gulf; roughly hourly Cairo connections.

Sharm El Sheikh.getting_in: SSH takes direct scheduled and charter flights from much of Europe and the Gulf year-round; from anywhere else, connect through Cairo (about 1h10 onward). The airport sits at the northern end of the resort strip — 15-25 minutes by road to Naama Bay, up to 40 minutes to Hadaba and the Old Market.

options.note: Pre-booked car to Naama Bay 15-25 min, about USD 8-15; farther bays up to 40 min.
Airport-rank taxis run roughly USD 10-15 to the central bays; agree the fare before getting in.
Many resorts include a minibus pickup with the room rate — confirm when booking.

car.note: Distances are short, taxis are everywhere, and Sinai police checkpoints plus assertive local driving make self-drive more hassle than help.

stay.why: Central pedestrian promenade of restaurants, bars and shops, with most dive-boat jetties a 10-minute ride away.
Clifftop quarter with house reefs off the rocks and quick access to the Old Market, noticeably quieter than Naama Bay.
Calm swimmable beach with a house reef, close to the jetties used by boats running to the Strait of Tiran.
Family all-inclusive strip 10 minutes from the airport fronting a broad, shallow, windswept lagoon.
The local end of town — cheaper rooms, Egyptian restaurants and the evening souk on foot.

todo.title: Mount Sinai sunrise climb and St Catherine's Monastery
todo.note: About 3 hours' drive to the trailhead, then a 2-3 hour night ascent to 2,285 m for sunrise. The 6th-century monastery below is closed to visitors on Sundays.

todo.title: Ras Mohammed National Park by land
todo.note: Jeep or bus tours cover the headland about 25 km south of town: snorkel bays, a mangrove channel and the inland salt lake, back by late afternoon.
todo.q: Ras Mohammed national park jeep tour

todo.title: White Island sandbar boat day
todo.note: Boats from Sharm El Maya anchor off a bare white sandbar inside Ras Mohammed for swimming and sunbathing, with snorkel stops on the way.

todo.title: Old Market evening and Al Sahaba Mosque
todo.note: Browse the spice and souvenir souk in Sharm El Maya, photograph the mosque's mixed Islamic-revival architecture, and eat grilled fish priced by the kilo.

todo.title: Quad bike sunset and Bedouin dinner
todo.note: Two-hour desert rides end at a Bedouin camp for tea, sand-baked bread and dinner under the stars; typically USD 25-40 per person.

todo.title: Colored Canyon day trip
todo.note: About 2.5 hours each way toward Nuweiba for a 1-2 hour scramble through a sandstone slot canyon with walls up to 40 m.

todo.title: Naama Bay promenade and SOHO Square by night
todo.note: Pedestrian strip of shisha cafes, bars and shops; SOHO Square adds fountains, an ice rink and family entertainment. Self-guided and safe on foot.

todo.title: Glass-bottom boat or semi-submarine
todo.note: One-hour reef viewing without getting wet — useful for non-diving companions and for your no-fly day before departure.

events.name: Ramadan
events.when: Moving dates, about 11 days earlier each year: 18 February-19 March in 2026, 7 February-9 March in 2027.
events.note: Resorts, boats and tourist restaurants run normally, but towns are quieter by day and lively after sunset; alcohol service can be limited outside hotels.

events.name: Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha holidays
events.when: Moving dates on the lunar calendar — Eid al-Fitr right after Ramadan, Eid al-Adha about 10 weeks later.
events.note: Egyptian holidaymakers fill Red Sea resorts for about a week each time; book rooms and domestic flights well ahead.

events.name: New Year peak week
events.when: Christmas through the first week of January, every year.
events.note: Peak pricing, full flights, and many resorts charge compulsory gala-dinner supplements on 31 December.

Sharm El Sheikh.topside_summary: A purpose-built resort strip along Sinai's southern tip: hotel bays, two promenade centres (Naama Bay and SOHO Square) and the Old Market souk, with bare desert mountains directly behind. It is also the gateway to the Sinai interior — canyon hikes, Bedouin camps and the Mount Sinai night climb all start here. Nightlife and shopping are concentrated and walkable; everything else is a short taxi ride.

airports.name: Hurghada International
airports.note: One of Egypt's busiest holiday airports — direct flights from most of Europe; also serves El Gouna, 25 km north.

Hurghada & El Gouna.getting_in: HRG has year-round direct scheduled and charter flights from most European cities plus frequent Cairo connections (about 1 hour). Hurghada's hotel zones start 10-15 minutes from the terminal; El Gouna has no airport of its own and is a 25 km, 30-45 minute road transfer north.

options.note: Pre-booked car to El Gouna 30-45 min, about EUR 17-25; central Hurghada 15-25 min, EUR 10-15.
Airport taxis ask EUR 20-25 to El Gouna and EUR 10-15 into town — fix the price before departure.
Inside El Gouna, tuk-tuks (a few euros per hop) and scheduled shuttle boats link Downtown, the lagoons and Abu Tig Marina.

car.note: El Gouna runs on tuk-tuks and shuttle boats, and Hurghada taxis are cheap; a rental only makes sense for repeated trips along the 40 km coastal strip.

stay.why: Planned lagoon town 25 km north of the airport — marina restaurants, calm swimmable lagoons, and everything reachable by tuk-tuk or shuttle boat.
Central Hurghada around the marina: walkable restaurants and nightlife, close to the jetties most dive boats leave from.
Hurghada's old town — souk, fish market and budget guesthouses, 20-30 minutes from the marina jetties.
Quiet planned resort bay about 20 km south with a long seafront boardwalk; suits couples wanting a beach base.
Self-contained family all-inclusive bay roughly 30 km south of the airport.

todo.title: Luxor day trip: Karnak and the Valley of the Kings
todo.note: About 280 km and 4 hours each way across the Eastern Desert; a 14-16 hour day starting 04:00-05:00 covering Karnak, the West Bank tombs and the Colossi of Memnon.
todo.q: Luxor day trip valley of the kings

todo.title: Giftun Island / Orange Bay beach day
todo.note: Boat day to white-sand islands 45-60 minutes offshore, with shallow turquoise swimming, snorkel stops and lunch on board.

todo.title: Quad or jeep desert safari with Bedouin dinner
todo.note: Afternoon rides through the Eastern Desert end at a camp for tea, bread baking and dinner as the sun drops behind the mountains.

todo.title: El Dahar souk and fish market
todo.note: Hurghada's old quarter: spice stalls, gold shops and a working fish market where nearby restaurants grill your pick by weight.

todo.title: El Gouna lagoon circuit by tuk-tuk and shuttle boat
todo.note: Ride the canals between Downtown and Abu Tig Marina, swim in a lagoon, and finish with dinner on the marina quay.

todo.title: Kitesurf taster lesson in El Gouna's lagoons
todo.note: Flat, waist-deep water and steady northerly wind make this one of the easiest places anywhere to try kitesurfing; 2-3 hour beginner sessions run year-round.

todo.title: Hurghada Grand Aquarium
todo.note: Walk-through tunnel aquarium and small zoo south of town; a reliable half day for kids or a windy afternoon.

todo.title: Sunset sailing cruise
todo.note: Two- to three-hour catamaran or felucca sails out of the marinas, usually with drinks and a swim stop.

todo.title: Abu Tig Marina evening
todo.note: El Gouna's yacht-lined restaurant row; bars and late cafes cluster here while Downtown stays more low-key.

events.name: El Gouna Film Festival
events.when: Nine days in mid-to-late October each year.
events.note: Open-air premieres and industry crowds fill El Gouna's hotels — book months ahead or stay in Hurghada and day-trip in.

events.name: El Gouna International Squash Open
events.when: About one week, usually April.
events.note: World-tour squash on an outdoor glass court by the marina; restaurants and hotels around Abu Tig get busy.

events.name: Ramadan
events.when: Moving dates, about 11 days earlier each year: 18 February-19 March in 2026, 7 February-9 March in 2027.
events.note: Resorts and El Gouna operate normally; Hurghada's local quarters are quieter by day and festive after sunset.

events.name: Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha holidays
events.when: Moving dates on the lunar calendar — Eid al-Fitr right after Ramadan, Eid al-Adha about 10 weeks later.
events.note: Domestic holiday surge for about a week each time; rooms and Cairo flights sell out early.

Hurghada & El Gouna.topside_summary: Hurghada is a working resort city — souk quarter, marina nightlife and a 40 km hotel strip — while El Gouna, 25 km north, is a privately planned lagoon town of bridges, tuk-tuks and quayside restaurants. Between them you get the widest topside range on the Egyptian Red Sea: island beach days, kitesurf lagoons, desert camps, and the only realistic day-trip base for Luxor.

airports.name: Sharm El Sheikh International
airports.note: Nearest airport, 90 km south of Dahab; nearly all visitors land here and transfer by road.

Dahab.getting_in: Dahab has no airport: fly into Sharm El Sheikh (direct European scheduled and charter flights, or via Cairo) and transfer 90 km north over the mountains, about 1 hour 15 minutes to 1.5 hours by road. Pre-booking the transfer is cheaper than negotiating at the terminal.

options.note: SSH to Dahab 1h15-1h30; roughly USD 25-45 pre-booked for a private car.
Airport-rank taxis ask around EUR 40-50 to Dahab, more at night; agree the fare first.
Shared minibuses and scheduled buses run Sharm-Dahab from the town bus station (about 1.5 h, a few euros) — you need a short taxi from arrivals first.

car.note: The town is walkable end-to-end along the seafront, excursions come with drivers, and Sinai road checkpoints make self-drive paperwork-heavy.

stay.why: The centre of town — seafront promenade of restaurants, cafes and guesthouses with everything on foot.
Quieter southern stretch of the same promenade; small hotels a few minutes' walk from the restaurant strip.
The local quarter behind the beach, with the cheapest long-stay rooms and everyday Egyptian eateries.
Sandy lagoon south of town where the kitesurf and windsurf schools sit; the closest Dahab gets to resort hotels.

todo.title: Blue Lagoon via Ras Abu Galum
todo.note: Jeep to the Blue Hole, then continue by camel, boat or a 2-3 hour coastal walk to a Bedouin-run lagoon of shallow turquoise water, with fresh fish lunch in a palm hut.

todo.title: Mount Sinai sunrise and St Catherine's Monastery
todo.note: About 2 hours' drive from Dahab, then a night ascent to 2,285 m for sunrise; the 6th-century monastery below is closed to visitors on Sundays.

todo.title: Colored Canyon hike
todo.note: Roughly six hours door to door: a drive toward Nuweiba, then a 1-2 hour walk through a sandstone slot canyon with 40 m walls.

todo.title: Wadi Gnai camel trek and Bedouin dinner
todo.note: Granite wadi 15 minutes south of town with acacias and rock pools; sunset camel rides end with sand-baked bread and dinner at a camp.

todo.title: Kitesurf or windsurf lesson at the Laguna
todo.note: The shallow sandy lagoon south of town has reliable wind most of the year; 2-3 hour beginner sessions are easy to book on the spot.

todo.title: Blue Hole rim for non-divers
todo.note: Carpeted Bedouin cafes ring the 100-m-deep sinkhole 30 minutes north of town; non-divers snorkel the coral lip or watch from the terraces with tea.

todo.title: Seafront promenade dinner and shopping
todo.note: Dinner on cushions at waterline restaurants, then the craft, spice and silver shops of Masbat — most of Dahab's evening happens along this 1.5 km strip.

todo.title: Quad biking and sandboarding in the dunes
todo.note: Short desert circuits into the wadis behind town, usually timed for sunset; often combined with a camp dinner.

events.name: Ramadan
events.when: Moving dates, about 11 days earlier each year: 18 February-19 March in 2026, 7 February-9 March in 2027.
events.note: Tourist restaurants and camps run normally; some local places close in daytime and evenings turn festive.

events.name: Eid holiday weeks
events.when: Moving dates — the week after Ramadan ends and again about 10 weeks later.
events.note: Cairo crowds fill Dahab's camps and guesthouses and room prices jump; book ahead or shift dates.

events.name: Freediving competition season
events.when: Editions cluster in autumn, with occasional spring events.
events.note: Competitions at the Blue Hole bring athletes and film crews; the site gets crowded on competition days and rooms in town tighten.

Dahab.topside_summary: A former Bedouin fishing village that grew into a low-rise, low-pressure beach town: carpeted waterline cafes, yoga-and-guesthouse lanes and granite mountains rising straight behind the palms. It is the cheapest and most laid-back of Egypt's Red Sea bases, with genuine Bedouin culture minutes from town. Nightlife means promenade dinners and beach fires, not clubs.

airports.name: Marsa Alam International
airports.note: 67 km north of Marsa Alam town and 10 minutes from Port Ghalib; seasonal European charters plus Cairo flights.

airports.name: Hurghada International
airports.note: Fallback with far more flight choice; 3.5-4 hours by road (about 215 km) to the northern Marsa Alam resorts.

Marsa Alam.getting_in: RMF is served by seasonal charters and scheduled flights from Europe plus near-daily Cairo connections (about 1h20). The airport is 10 minutes from Port Ghalib but 45-90 minutes from most south-coast resorts; travellers who can't route into RMF fly to Hurghada and transfer 3.5-4 hours south by road.

options.note: RMF to Port Ghalib 10-15 min (about EUR 10-25); to Marsa Alam town or southern resorts 45-90 min, EUR 25-50 pre-booked.
Local taxis run about EUR 8-15 to Port Ghalib; fares south rise with distance — agree before setting off.
Most liveaboards depart Port Ghalib marina and include or arrange the 10-minute airport pickup.

car.note: Resorts are strung along 100-plus km of coast with sparse fuel and services in the far south; hotel transfers and arranged drivers are how everyone moves.

stay.why: Marina village 10 minutes from the airport with restaurants, a promenade and the main liveaboard departure quay.
Resort cluster around sheltered bays where green turtles graze seagrass within swimming distance of the beach.
Small working town 67 km south of the airport; budget guesthouses and dive camps rather than resorts.
Coral-stone old town with a 16th-century fort about 60 km north of Port Ghalib; quiet mid-range hotels.
Remote resorts 120 km-plus south near Wadi el Gemal, chosen for reefs and quiet rather than amenities.

todo.title: Wadi el Gemal National Park safari
todo.note: Jeep and camel routes through a desert-and-mangrove national park about 50 km south of town: Roman-era emerald mine settlements, gazelle, ibex and a bird-rich coast.

todo.title: Abu Dabbab bay swim with turtles
todo.note: Walk-in sandy bay about 30 km north of Marsa Alam town where green turtles graze seagrass metres from shore; dugong sightings happen but are rare. Sunbeds and showers on the beach.

todo.title: El Quseir old town and fort
todo.note: A 16th-century fort (now a small museum) and lanes of coral-block houses roughly an hour north of Port Ghalib; go late afternoon when the souk street wakes up.

todo.title: Luxor trip across the desert
todo.note: Karnak and the Valley of the Kings are 4-5 hours each way from the northern resorts — feasible as one very long day, better as an overnight.

todo.title: Bedouin dinner and stargazing in the Eastern Desert
todo.note: Inland camps run camel rides, sand-baked bread and telescope sessions; this far from any city the sky is genuinely dark.

todo.title: Port Ghalib marina promenade
todo.note: The marina's restaurant row and evening bazaar fill rest days and pre-flight afternoons, with berthed liveaboards to size up for next time.

todo.title: Quad bike sunset safari
todo.note: Two- to three-hour circuits into the desert behind the coast road, timed for sunset and often paired with a camp dinner.

todo.title: Sataya reef dolphin day from Hamata
todo.note: From the far south, boats anchor at a crescent reef where spinner dolphin pods rest; in-water viewing is regulated, so keep expectations flexible.

events.name: Ramadan
events.when: Moving dates, about 11 days earlier each year: 18 February-19 March in 2026, 7 February-9 March in 2027.
events.note: Resorts and boats run normally; Marsa Alam town and El Quseir are quiet by day and lively after sunset.

events.name: Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha holidays
events.when: Moving dates on the lunar calendar — the week after Ramadan and again about 10 weeks later.
events.note: Domestic holiday surge; transfers and the few restaurants outside resorts get busy, and Cairo flights fill.

events.name: Green turtle nesting season
events.when: Roughly June to September each year on southern beaches.
events.note: Some beach sections are roped off at night; early risers may see tracks or hatchlings — keep lights off and keep your distance.

Marsa Alam.topside_summary: The quietest of Egypt's Red Sea bases: a thin line of resorts along 100-plus km of desert coast, anchored by the Port Ghalib marina and the small working town of Marsa Alam. Topside life is desert — national-park safaris, Bedouin camps, very dark skies — plus the coral-stone old town of El Quseir an hour north. People come for the reefs and the space, not for nightlife.

airports.name: Phuket International
airports.note: Direct long-haul and regional arrivals; the airport sits at the island's north tip, 30-45 km from most beach areas.

Phuket.getting_in: Fly straight into HKT on a direct international service, or connect through Bangkok on the roughly 1 h 25 min domestic hop. Most dive boats leave from Chalong Pier in the island's south, about an hour's drive from the airport.

options.note: Official airport taxis run 800-1,000 THB to Patong including the 100 THB airport surcharge; 45-60 minutes depending on traffic.
Pre-booked cars use fixed zone pricing (roughly 800-1,200 THB to the west-coast beaches) with a driver meeting you in arrivals.
Ride-hailing apps operate island-wide and usually undercut the taxi counters; pickup points at the airport are signposted.
The airport smart bus follows the west coast to Rawai for about 100 THB per person, hourly, 60-90 minutes to Patong.

car.note: Traffic on the airport-Patong corridor is heavy, parking near the beaches is tight, and Thailand drives on the left. Scooters dominate local traffic and rental accidents involving visitors are common; taxis and apps cover the island well enough.

stay.why: Closest base to Chalong Pier, where most dive-boat and liveaboard departures leave from, with a quieter local-restaurant scene.
Long swimmable beaches and a mid-paced resort strip, 20-30 minutes from Chalong Pier for morning boat pickups.
Shophouse architecture, coffee shops and the island's best food, at the cost of a 30-40 minute drive to any beach.
The nightlife and shopping hub with the widest hotel choice; loud, but dive pickups service it daily.

todo.title: Walk Phuket Old Town
todo.note: Sino-Portuguese shophouses along Thalang and Dibuk Roads, street art, and Peranakan-style cafes. Sunday evenings Thalang Road closes to traffic for a walking-street market.

todo.title: Visit the Big Buddha on Nakkerd Hill
todo.note: The 45 m white marble statue above Chalong reopened to visitors in March 2026 after a long closure; open 09:00-18:00, free entry, wide views over both coasts.

todo.title: Wat Chalong temple
todo.note: Phuket's largest Buddhist temple complex, a 10-minute drive from Chalong Pier, so it pairs easily with a boat day. Cover shoulders and knees.

todo.title: Sea-kayak Phang Nga Bay
todo.note: Paddle through the hongs (collapsed sea caves) inside the bay's limestone karst islands, entered at low tide through tunnel passages. Day trips run year-round from the island's east side.
todo.q: Phang Nga Bay sea kayak hong

todo.title: Night-market crawl
todo.note: The weekend market near Central Festival and the nightly Chillva market in Phuket Town cover grilled seafood, southern Thai curries and 20-60 THB street plates.

todo.title: Watch or train Muay Thai
todo.note: Stadiums around Patong and Chalong hold fight nights several evenings a week, and gyms run drop-in beginner sessions of 1-2 hours.

todo.title: Thai cooking class
todo.note: Half-day courses typically start with a wet-market visit then cook 4-5 dishes such as tom yam and massaman. A practical surface-interval activity between dive days.

todo.title: Sunset at Promthep Cape
todo.note: The headland at the island's southern tip is the standard sunset stop; arrive an hour early for parking and walk down the ridge trail away from the crowds.

todo.title: Ethical elephant sanctuary visit
todo.note: Several rescue sanctuaries in the island's interior offer observation-based visits with feeding and no riding. Check that the one you pick keeps a no-riding, no-bathing policy.

todo.title: Beach day at Nai Harn or Freedom Beach
todo.note: Nai Harn has calm high-season swimming and shade; Freedom Beach requires a longtail ride or steep walk in, which keeps numbers down.

events.name: Phuket Vegetarian Festival
events.when: Nine days in the ninth Chinese lunar month, usually late September to mid-October (10-18 October in 2026)
events.note: Street processions with firewalking and body-piercing rituals centre on Phuket Town's shrines; roads close, firecrackers run all day, and hotels in town fill. Excellent food stalls flying yellow flags serve vegan southern Thai dishes.

events.name: Songkran
events.when: 13-15 April, Thai New Year
events.note: Island-wide water fights, especially in Patong and Old Town; transport books out and prices rise. Keep cameras and phones in dry bags anywhere near a road.

events.name: Loy Krathong
events.when: Full moon of the twelfth Thai lunar month, usually mid-to-late November
events.note: Locals float candle-lit krathong baskets at beaches and lagoons after dark; a calm, photogenic evening rather than a disruption.

Phuket.topside_summary: Phuket is Thailand's largest island and its most developed resort economy, so the topside mix runs from Michelin-listed southern Thai food and Sino-Portuguese heritage streets in Phuket Town to full-volume nightlife in Patong. The interior and south keep temples, viewpoints and quieter beaches within a 30-minute drive of the dive piers. It works best as a base where non-diving partners and families have as much to do as the divers.

airports.name: Samui International
airports.note: Fastest route: fly here then take the 1.5-2 h high-speed catamaran to Koh Tao.

airports.name: Chumphon
airports.note: Budget domestic route; the mainland pier with the shortest crossing (about 1 h 45 min) to Koh Tao.

airports.name: Suvarnabhumi
airports.note: Main international gateway; connect by domestic flight, or overnight bus/train plus ferry combo.

Koh Tao.getting_in: Koh Tao has no airport: fly Bangkok to Koh Samui (USM) or Chumphon (CJM) and connect to a high-speed ferry into Mae Haad Pier, giving a total door-to-door time of roughly 4-6 hours. The cheaper classic route is an overnight bus or train from Bangkok to Chumphon (or slower via Surat Thani) sold as a combined ticket with the morning ferry, about 10-11 hours in total.

options.note: High-speed catamarans from Chumphon take about 1 h 45 min (roughly 600-700 THB); from Koh Samui or Koh Phangan about 1.5-2.5 h (from about 550-700 THB). Book ahead December-March; crossings get rough and are occasionally cancelled in the November monsoon.
Combined bus-plus-ferry tickets from Bangkok run overnight to Chumphon and connect to the morning boat, 10-11 hours total.
On the island itself, shared pickup-truck taxis and longtail water taxis link Mae Haad Pier with Sairee and the southern bays; agree the fare before boarding.

car.note: There is no practical way or reason to bring a car; the island is 21 km2 with steep, partly unpaved concrete roads. Locals and visitors move by scooter (left-hand traffic, frequent rental-damage disputes and accidents), shared pickup taxi, longtail boat or on foot.

stay.why: The 1.7 km west-coast strip holds most restaurants, bars and sunset viewing, with everything walkable.
The ferry-pier village; practical for early boats and short stays, with a calmer evening scene than Sairee.
The sheltered southern bay is the quiet option, 10 minutes by taxi from the pier and handy for the south-coast viewpoints.

todo.title: Koh Nang Yuan viewpoint
todo.note: A longtail taxi boat from Sairee (about 350 THB return) reaches the three-islet group; the short stair climb tops out above the connecting sandbar. Entry fee applies and the island closes late afternoon.

todo.title: Hike to John-Suwan viewpoint
todo.note: A steep 15-20 minute scramble from the south end of Freedom Beach to a rock platform over Chalok Baan Kao and Shark Bay. Go early morning for light and cooler air.

todo.title: Coastal walk Mae Haad to Sai Nuan
todo.note: An unmarked shoreline path links Jansom Bay and the two small Sai Nuan beaches, which stay near-empty on weekday mornings. Bring water; there is little shade on the connecting sections.

todo.title: Kayak the east-coast bays
todo.note: Rent a kayak or paddleboard and work along Tanote Bay and Ao Leuk, granite-boulder coves that are awkward to reach by road. Afternoon sea breeze picks up, so paddle out early.

todo.title: Rock climbing and bouldering
todo.note: The island's rounded granite gives friction climbing unlike Thailand's limestone crags, with guided half-day sessions and bolted routes near Tanote Bay suitable for beginners.

todo.title: Sairee sunset and bar strip
todo.note: The west-facing beach lines up directly with the sunset; beach bars run fire shows and open decks from about 17:30. Low-key by Thai island standards, which suits early dive starts.

todo.title: Thai cooking class
todo.note: Small-group classes in Sairee and Mae Haad cook 3-5 standard dishes in 3-4 hours, a reliable option for a no-fly day before departure.

events.name: Full Moon Party (Koh Phangan)
events.when: Every full moon, on neighbouring Koh Phangan
events.note: Not on Koh Tao itself, but ferries and accommodation across the gulf islands fill for 2-3 days around each full moon; some visitors day-trip over and dive groups thin out the morning after.

events.name: Songkran
events.when: 13-15 April, Thai New Year
events.note: Water fights along Sairee's main lane; ferries and rooms book out as Thai holidaymakers travel, so reserve crossings early.

events.name: Loy Krathong
events.when: Full moon of the twelfth Thai lunar month, usually mid-to-late November
events.note: Candle-lit floats launched from Sairee Beach after dark. November is also the gulf's rainy month, when ferry cancellations are most likely.

Koh Tao.topside_summary: Koh Tao is a 21 km2 granite island built almost entirely around its dive economy, with a compact strip of restaurants and beach bars at Sairee and quiet coves everywhere else. Off-gassing days fill easily with viewpoint hikes, kayaking and empty south-coast beaches rather than shopping or big-ticket sights. It suits travellers happy with a small-island rhythm; anyone wanting resorts and variety should pair it with Koh Samui.

airports.name: Phuket International
airports.note: The gateway airport; Khao Lak, the mainland base for the Similans, is 60-80 km north by road.

Similan Islands.getting_in: There is no airport or overnight accommodation for visitors in the islands themselves: fly to Phuket (HKT) and transfer about 1-1.5 hours north to Khao Lak. Boats leave from Thap Lamu Pier, a 15-minute drive south of Khao Lak town, either as multi-day liveaboards or 1-1.5 hour speedboat day trips.

options.note: Pre-booked car from HKT to Khao Lak costs roughly 1,200-1,800 THB and takes 75-90 minutes; to Thap Lamu Pier allow up to 2 hours.
Most liveaboards include or arrange minibus pickup from Phuket or Khao Lak hotels to Thap Lamu, typically boarding in the evening and crossing to the islands overnight.
Day-trip speedboats cover Thap Lamu to the islands in 1-1.5 hours; they run only during the open season, roughly mid-October to mid-May.

car.note: Boat operators handle pier pickups, so a car is unnecessary for the diving. Khao Lak itself is a 20 km strip where a rental (left-hand driving, easy highway) is only worth it if you plan self-drive day trips to Khao Sok or Takua Pa; otherwise taxis and hotel shuttles cover it.

stay.why: The main services cluster with dive shops, restaurants and pharmacies, closest to Thap Lamu Pier for early boat departures.
A slightly quieter beach stretch 3 km north with the area's best night market and a wide, walkable sand beach.
Low-density resort beaches north of town for travellers who want quiet between liveaboard legs; you will need transport for restaurants.

todo.title: Day trip to Khao Sok and Cheow Lan Lake
todo.note: The rainforest park is about 1.5 hours inland, with longtail-boat trips among limestone towers on the reservoir and trails where hornbills and gibbons are regularly heard. A standard add-on before or after a liveaboard.
todo.q: Khao Sok Cheow Lan lake day trip

todo.title: Kayak the Little Amazon mangroves
todo.note: Guided inflatable-kayak floats through the banyan and mangrove channel at Takua Pa run about an hour on the water (around 500 THB) with frequent sightings of snakes, monitor lizards and macaques.
todo.q: Little Amazon mangrove kayak Takua Pa

todo.title: Takua Pa old town
todo.note: A former tin-mining town 30 km north with preserved Sino-Portuguese arcade shophouses and street art; liveliest during the Sunday afternoon walking-street market roughly October to May.
todo.q: Takua Pa old town walking street

todo.title: Bang Niang market and the beached patrol boat
todo.note: The afternoon-to-evening market (Monday, Wednesday, Thursday, Saturday) covers grilled seafood and fruit stalls; the police patrol boat carried 1 km inland by the 2004 tsunami stands nearby as a memorial.
todo.q: Bang Niang night market tsunami memorial

todo.title: Waterfalls and coast trails of Khao Lak-Lam Ru
todo.note: The small national park behind town has short jungle trails, the multi-tier Ton Chong Fa waterfall (best just after the wet season) and a headland viewpoint over the coast.
todo.q: Khao Lak Lam Ru national park waterfall hike

todo.title: Beach afternoon on Bang Niang or Bangsak
todo.note: Long, flat, west-facing sand with calm high-season swimming and few vendors; sunset lines up directly offshore toward the islands.

events.name: Similan National Park seasonal closure
events.when: Approximately 16 May to 14 October each year
events.note: The park shuts completely for the southwest monsoon, so no day trips or liveaboards operate; plan Similan diving between mid-October and mid-May and confirm the exact dates for your year.

events.name: Season opening
events.when: Around 15 October
events.note: Boats resume in the first open weeks with the fewest visitors of the season, though sea conditions can still be mixed into early November.

events.name: Loy Krathong
events.when: Full moon of the twelfth Thai lunar month, usually mid-to-late November
events.note: Krathong floats are launched from Khao Lak's beaches and at Bang Niang market in the evening; no travel disruption.

events.name: Songkran
events.when: 13-15 April, Thai New Year
events.note: Water fights along the Khao Lak main road and busy domestic travel in the season's final open month; book transfers and rooms ahead.

Similan Islands.topside_summary: The Similans themselves are uninhabited granite islands inside a national park, so the topside experience belongs to Khao Lak, the low-rise mainland beach town where the boats are based. Khao Lak trades nightlife for long quiet beaches, afternoon markets and quick access to rainforest at Khao Sok and the mangrove channels of Takua Pa. It suits couples and families pacing a liveaboard with slow beach days, and nearly everything closes down to a local rhythm during the May-October park closure.

airports.name: Cozumel International
airports.note: On-island; year-round nonstops from Dallas, Miami and Houston, plus seasonal routes from Atlanta, Chicago, Charlotte, Denver and Minneapolis.

airports.name: Cancún International
airports.note: Far more flight choice and often cheaper; connect via ground transfer to Playa del Carmen and the passenger ferry.

Cozumel.getting_in: Fly direct into CZM if your city has a route; otherwise land at CUN, transfer about 1 hour south to Playa del Carmen and take the passenger ferry across (about 40 minutes, departures roughly every 30-60 minutes until late evening).

options.note: Shared vans from CZM airport run about 10-20 USD per person depending on hotel zone; the airport is only 3-4 km from downtown San Miguel.
Airport taxis cost roughly 15-35 USD by zone; around town, fixed-rate taxis are the default and are easy to flag down.
Playa del Carmen-Cozumel passenger ferry, about 40 minutes, roughly 220-290 MXN (13-16 USD) one way; two competing operators alternate departures.

car.note: Taxis cover the town and west-coast hotel strip fine. Rent a car, jeep or scooter for one day (roughly 25-80 USD) to loop the wild east coast and Punta Sur — that single day trip is the main reason to drive here.

stay.why: Walkable to the ferry pier, the malecón, taco stands and most morning boat departures, with the island's best-value hotels.
A quieter strip of mid-size hotels and condos 2-5 km north of town, with calm shore entries and sunset-facing pools.
Resorts strung down the southwest coast sit closest to the southern reefs, so boat rides are shortest — but you depend on taxis for town.

todo.title: San Gervasio ruins
todo.note: Cozumel's main Maya site, a sanctuary of the fertility goddess Ixchel where mainland women once made pilgrimage. Combined entry runs about 14-15 USD; jungle paths link several small temple groups. Open 8:00-16:00.

todo.title: Punta Sur eco park
todo.note: Reserve at the island's southern tip with a 133-step lighthouse climb, a crocodile lagoon and an empty white beach. Arrive at 09:00 opening to catch crocodiles and iguanas before the heat; entry about 20 USD, closed Sundays.

todo.title: East-coast road loop
todo.note: The undeveloped windward coast is one road, a string of surf beaches like Chen Río and Punta Morena, and a handful of ramshackle beach bars. Swim only in the protected coves — the open surf has strong currents.

todo.title: El Cielo sandbar by boat
todo.note: A shallow sandbar off the southwest coast, 1-2 m deep and scattered with sea stars, reached only by boat trips that usually pair it with a Palancar snorkel stop. An easy surface day for off-gassing before a flight.
todo.q: El Cielo sandbar boat trip Cozumel

todo.title: Beach clubs at Playa Palancar
todo.note: Low-key sand-floor beach clubs on the southwest coast where a lounger costs the price of lunch. Calm water, palm shade and ceviche; go on a non-cruise-ship day for space.

todo.title: San Miguel food crawl
todo.note: Skip the waterfront tourist rows and eat where the island does: cochinita pibil tortas at the Mercado Municipal, late-night taco stands along Avenida 30 and coffee roasters on the plaza side streets.
todo.q: San Miguel Cozumel local tacos market

todo.title: Island museum on the malecón
todo.note: Compact two-floor museum covering reef ecology, Maya settlement and the island's 20th-century chicle and dive history — useful context for what you see underwater. An hour is enough.

todo.title: El Cedral village
todo.note: The island's oldest settlement, a tiny inland village with a small Maya ruin and a church, quiet outside its late-April festival. Pair it with the Palancar beaches 10 minutes away.

todo.title: Sunset on the malecón
todo.note: The seafront promenade faces due west toward the mainland; locals gather at the plaza on Sunday evenings for food carts and live music. Rooftop bars along the strip catch the last light.

events.name: Carnaval de Cozumel
events.when: The week before Lent, usually mid-February (Feb 11-18 in 2026), with parades on the final Saturday, Sunday and Fat Tuesday
events.note: The island's biggest party, running over 150 years; the malecón closes for night parades, hotels fill and the town is loud until late. Book well ahead or aim for the festive pre-Carnaval weeks.

events.name: El Cedral Festival
events.when: Roughly April 28 to May 3, ending on the Day of the Holy Cross
events.note: A 170-year-old fair in El Cedral village with rodeos, horse races, dancing and roast-meat feasts. Easy to add to a dive week; taxis and rental cars reach the village in 20 minutes from town.

events.name: Ironman Cozumel
events.when: Late November (Nov 22 in 2026)
events.note: A full-distance triathlon that closes the coastal road and fills hotels for the weekend; the swim course sits over the reefs. Avoid the dates unless you're racing, or book far ahead.

events.name: Día de Muertos
events.when: October 31 to November 2
events.note: Altars appear in the plaza, cemeteries and shopfronts of San Miguel; a small, local-feeling version of the holiday rather than a spectacle. No disruption to diving.

events.name: Sargassum season
events.when: May through September, worst June-August
events.note: Cozumel's west-facing leeward coast is largely spared by the island's geography, so beaches here stay far cleaner than the mainland's — a real advantage for a summer trip.

Cozumel.topside_summary: Cozumel is a small-town island: one real settlement, San Miguel, a west coast of piers and beach clubs, and a windward east side that is still empty road and surf. Topside days revolve around a rental-jeep coast loop, Maya ruins in the scrub jungle, and unhurried meals a few blocks back from the waterfront. Cruise ships bring day crowds to the pier zone, but evenings belong to the plaza.

airports.name: Cancún International
airports.note: The region's main hub with four terminals and nonstops from dozens of North American and European cities; 20 minutes from the Hotel Zone, about an hour from Playa del Carmen.

Cancún & Playa del Carmen.getting_in: Nearly everyone lands at CUN, the busiest gateway in the Mexican Caribbean. Cancún's Hotel Zone is 20-30 minutes from the terminal; Playa del Carmen is a 55-90 minute run south on highway 307 by bus or pre-booked van, and Playa's pier is the ferry link to Cozumel.

options.note: Direct first-class buses leave each CUN terminal for downtown Playa del Carmen roughly every 30 minutes, about 270 MXN (15 USD), 1 h 15 min to 1 h 50 min.
Pre-booked vans (up to 6-8 people) run about 45-90 USD one way to Playa del Carmen, 55-60 minutes door to door; the sensible choice with dive bags.
Authorized airport taxis are metered by zone and cost more than pre-booked vans; note that app rideshares cannot pick up inside the airport.
From Playa del Carmen's pier, the Cozumel passenger ferry takes about 40 minutes; from Puerto Juárez in Cancún, the Isla Mujeres ferry takes 15-20 minutes.

car.note: Not needed if you're basing in the Hotel Zone or central Playa — both are walkable and buses/colectivos ply highway 307 constantly. Rent for a day or two only if you want self-paced cenote or ruin trips.

stay.why: A 22 km barrier-island strip of big beach resorts between the Caribbean and the lagoon — the widest sand and the shortest airport run.
Markets, taquerías and hotels at half Hotel Zone prices, 15 minutes by bus from the beach and close to the Isla Mujeres ferry docks.
Walk to the Cozumel ferry, dive piers and the Quinta Avenida restaurant strip; the most practical base for mixing reef days with mainland trips.
A quiet gated enclave of resorts and villas immediately south of Playa's center — beach access and calm nights, ten minutes' walk from the action.

todo.title: Isla Mujeres day trip
todo.note: Ferries cross from Puerto Juárez in 15-20 minutes; rent a golf cart (roughly 40-90 USD/day) and loop the 8 km island from Playa Norte's shallow sandbar to the sculpture park and cliffs at the southern point.
todo.q: Isla Mujeres golf cart day trip

todo.title: Chichén Itzá
todo.note: The peninsula's headline Maya city, about 2.5-3 hours west; foreign adult entry totals roughly 700 MXN (about 40 USD) and gates run 08:00-17:00. Leave by 06:30 or stay in Valladolid to beat tour-bus crowds and heat.

todo.title: Tulum clifftop ruins
todo.note: A walled Maya port on 12 m cliffs above turquoise water, about an hour south of Playa; combined fees total roughly 515 MXN. Go for the 08:00 opening — by 10:00 the site is packed.

todo.title: Cenote swim day
todo.note: Open-air swimming cenotes like Cenote Azul and Jardín del Edén sit just off highway 307 south of Playa, with entries around 150-200 MXN. Clear fresh water, cliff-jump ledges and fish — an easy no-dive afternoon.

todo.title: Akumal turtle snorkel
todo.note: Green turtles graze the seagrass in Akumal Bay, 40 minutes south of Playa. Turtle-zone snorkeling now requires a licensed guide booked at the beach modules (about 25-40 USD, 55-minute sessions, 3 m distance rule).

todo.title: Quinta Avenida evening
todo.note: Playa del Carmen's 4 km pedestrian spine runs from the ferry pier north past taquerías, mezcal bars and rooftop lounges. Eat a block or two off the avenue where prices drop and the cooking improves.
todo.q: Quinta Avenida Playa del Carmen restaurants

todo.title: Whale shark snorkel trip
todo.note: From June to mid-September hundreds of whale sharks feed off Isla Mujeres and Holbox; boats leave Cancún and Isla Mujeres early morning for 1-1.5 hour rides to the aggregation. Snorkel only — no tanks — with peak numbers in July-August.

todo.title: Maya Museum of Cancún
todo.note: A modern museum in the Hotel Zone holding one of the region's most complete Maya collections, with the small San Miguelito ruins in the jungle out back. A solid rainy-afternoon or surface-interval plan.

todo.title: Downtown Cancún market eating
todo.note: Mercado 28 and the Parque de las Palapas food stalls serve the city that tourism built — salbutes, marquesitas and grilled corn at local prices, best in the evening when families come out.

todo.title: Xpu-Ha beach afternoon
todo.note: A long, palm-backed bay 25 minutes south of Playa with a few low-rise beach clubs and calmer water than town beaches. Reachable by colectivo along highway 307 plus a short walk in.

events.name: Whale shark season
events.when: June to mid-September, peaking July-August
events.note: The world's largest known whale shark aggregation forms offshore; morning boats sell out in peak weeks, so book a day or two ahead and keep a buffer day for weather.

events.name: Sargassum season
events.when: Roughly April to October, heaviest June-August
events.note: Drifting seaweed can pile up on east-facing mainland beaches; resorts rake daily and amounts vary week to week. Diving is unaffected, and November-February beaches are most reliably clear.

events.name: Día de Muertos / Hanal Pixán
events.when: End of October through November 2
events.note: The Maya version of Day of the Dead fills plazas in Cancún and Playa with altars, candlelit processions and festival nights at the big eco-parks south of Playa. Book those festival evenings ahead.

events.name: Carnaval
events.when: The week before Lent, usually mid-February
events.note: Playa del Carmen and Cancún both stage parades and street concerts in the same window as Cozumel's larger Carnaval — lively evenings, minor street closures.

events.name: Spring break
events.when: March, concentrated in the Cancún Hotel Zone
events.note: US college crowds push Hotel Zone prices up and noise levels higher; Playa del Carmen and points south feel it far less.

Cancún & Playa del Carmen.topside_summary: This is the high-energy end of the Mexican Caribbean: Cancún's resort strip and nightlife on one end, Playa del Carmen's walkable grid of restaurants, ferries and beach clubs on the other, with highway 307 threading cenotes, turtle bays and Maya ruins between them. Everything runs on day trips — you can climb a pyramid, swim a cenote and eat street tacos in the same day. It is busy and commercial, but the infrastructure makes non-diving days effortless.

airports.name: Cancún International
airports.note: Main gateway with the most flights and cheapest fares; 1 hour to Playa del Carmen, 1.5-2 hours to Tulum on highway 307.

airports.name: Tulum International (Felipe Carrillo Puerto)
airports.note: Opened December 2023, about 25 km south of Tulum town; a growing set of US nonstops (Dallas, Houston, Miami, Atlanta, Chicago) puts you 35-45 minutes from a Tulum base.

Cenotes of Yucatán Peninsula.getting_in: The cenote belt runs along highway 307 between Playa del Carmen and Tulum and inland on the Cobá road, so most divers land at CUN and drive south; TQO is the shortcut if you're basing in Tulum. There is no single 'cenote town' — Playa del Carmen and Tulum are the two practical bases.

options.note: First-class buses run from CUN's terminals to Playa del Carmen (about 270 MXN, 1 h 15 min-1 h 50 min) and on to Tulum; colectivo vans shuttle the 307 corridor all day for a few dollars a hop.
Pre-booked vans from CUN cost about 45-90 USD to Playa del Carmen and roughly 100-140 USD to Tulum (1.5-2 h) — worth it with cave-diving luggage.
Taxis cover in-town runs in Playa and Tulum; for cenote gates 20-45 km out they get expensive fast and you'll need to arrange the return.

car.note: This is the classic rental-car destination: cenotes are scattered down 60 km of highway 307 and the Cobá road, gates open around 08:00-09:00 when the water is clearest and emptiest, and a car lets you chain two or three sites a day. Budget for mandatory third-party liability insurance on top of teaser base rates, and carry cash — most cenote gates take pesos only.

stay.why: The budget-friendly grid on the highway side of Tulum, closest to the Cobá-road cenotes and full of taquerías; you trade beach frontage for location and price.
The boho hotel strip along the sand — atmospheric and expensive, with a 10-20 minute drive back inland to most cenote gates.
The most connected base: cenotes south toward Tulum, the Cozumel ferry for reef days, and buses in every direction from one walkable center.
Quiet marina-and-bay communities midway along the cenote belt, cutting drive times to popular systems roughly in half versus either town.

todo.title: Cobá pyramid climb
todo.note: Nohoch Mul, 42 m tall, reopened to climbers in December 2025 via a new wooden staircase over the original 120 steps — again the only major pyramid in the peninsula you can ascend. Climbing runs 08:00-15:30 in small groups; rent a bike to cover the jungle site's long causeways.

todo.title: Tulum clifftop ruins
todo.note: The walled Maya port stands on 12 m cliffs over the Caribbean; combined entry fees total about 515 MXN and last admission is 15:30. Arrive at 08:00 — it is the most-visited site on the coast.

todo.title: Chichén Itzá via Valladolid
todo.note: About 2 hours inland from Tulum; pair the ruins (roughly 700 MXN foreign entry, 08:00 opening) with lunch in Valladolid, a pastel colonial town with a swimmable cenote right in its center. Doing it self-drive beats the bus-tour timetable.

todo.title: Swim-cenote circuit for non-divers
todo.note: Open cenotes like Gran Cenote and Cenote Azul welcome swimmers and snorkelers for about 150-400 MXN each — turtles, light shafts and cool 25 °C water. Guided snorkel routes through the Dos Ojos caverns (about 370-700 MXN) give non-divers a taste of the cave systems.

todo.title: Sian Ka'an lagoon float at Muyil
todo.note: From the Muyil ruins, boats cross two lagoons in the UNESCO biosphere to a Maya-dug canal where you drift with the current through the mangroves in a life vest. Half-day trips run about 50-100 USD; over 300 bird species live in the reserve.

todo.title: Akumal turtle bay
todo.note: Green turtles feed on the seagrass a short swim off Akumal beach; guided turtle-zone sessions (mandatory, about 25-40 USD, 55 minutes) are booked at the beach entrance. The rule set — 3 m distance, no touching, reef-safe sunscreen — is enforced.

todo.title: Punta Laguna monkey reserve
todo.note: A community-run forest reserve 40 minutes past Cobá where local guides track wild spider monkey troops around a lagoon; early morning gives the best sightings. A low-key counterpoint to the coast's commercial parks.

todo.title: Tulum centro taco crawl
todo.note: The town-side avenues serve al pastor, cochinita and seafood tostadas at a third of beach-strip prices, with mezcalerías filling in after dark. Eat where the pickup trucks park.

todo.title: Playa Paraíso beach afternoon
todo.note: The wide public beach just south of the Tulum ruins, with fine sand, beach-club loungers and a view back to the clifftop temples. Check seaweed conditions in summer months before committing a day.

todo.title: Kaan Luum lagoon
todo.note: A shallow turquoise lagoon 10 minutes south of Tulum ringing a deep central cenote; entry is cheap, docks and swings line the shallows, and weekday mornings are near-empty. Swimming is allowed in the shallow ring only.

events.name: Día de Muertos / Hanal Pixán
events.when: End of October through November 2
events.note: The Yucatec Maya version of Day of the Dead — altars with 'food for the souls' appear in Tulum, Valladolid and every village square, and the coast's eco-parks stage multi-night festivals. One of the best culture windows of the year.

events.name: Equinox at Chichén Itzá
events.when: Around March 20-21 and September 22, strongest in March
events.note: The serpent-shadow illusion descends El Castillo's staircase and draws tens of thousands; a spectacle if you want it, a reason to shift your visit by a few days if you don't.

events.name: Sargassum season
events.when: Roughly April to October, heaviest June-August; Tulum's open coast collects the most of any beach on this coast
events.note: Beach days suffer in peak months, but cenotes are completely unaffected — freshwater, inland and clear year-round — which makes this destination a smart summer pick.

events.name: Whale shark season
events.when: June to mid-September off Isla Mujeres and Holbox, peak July-August
events.note: An add-on day from a cenote trip: leave at dawn from Cancún or Isla Mujeres, snorkel with the aggregation, and be back on the 307 corridor by evening.

events.name: Rainy season
events.when: June through October, with short heavy afternoon storms and the hurricane watch peaking August-October
events.note: Rain rarely wrecks a cenote day — most storms pass in an hour and the caverns don't care — but heavy runoff can temporarily cloud open cenotes' surface layers.

Cenotes of Yucatán Peninsula.topside_summary: The cenote belt is jungle-and-highway country: hundreds of collapsed limestone sinkholes strung between Playa del Carmen, Tulum and the Cobá road, with Maya cities, monkey forest and the Sian Ka'an biosphere folded in among them. Topside life means a rental car, cash for village gates, taco stops between swims, and ruins climbed before the heat. Tulum adds a beach-club scene; inland, the peninsula stays rural and unhurried.

airports.name: Velana International
airports.note: On Hulhulé island, linked to Malé by bridge; all onward atoll transfers start here

airports.name: Gan International
airports.note: Far-south gateway, mostly reached by domestic connection from MLE

Maldives.getting_in: Long-haul flights land at MLE (Velana International), on its own island next to Malé. From there every atoll leg is a second booking — resort speedboat, seaplane (daylight hours only), or a domestic flight plus boat for the far atolls — arranged through your resort or guesthouse.

options.note: Resort speedboats cover islands within about 80 km of Malé in 20-60 minutes; typically 100-200 USD return per person. Scheduled shared boats to local islands like Maafushi run about 25-30 USD one way, 45 minutes.

Seaplanes serve mid-distance atolls, roughly 400-820 USD return per person, flying only about 06:00-16:00 — late arrivals overnight near the airport. Far atolls use a domestic flight plus speedboat, about 350-520 USD return.
Public ferries link Malé with local islands for 2-4 USD; Malé-Maafushi takes about 90 minutes and does not run on Fridays.
Liveaboards collect guests directly at the airport jetty or nearby Hulhumalé marina, usually on embarkation afternoon.

car.note: There is no road network between islands; apart from the Malé-Hulhumalé bridge, everything moves by boat or air, so a car is pointless.

stay.why: One island, one resort: transfers, meals, house reef and alcohol all on site, with prices running from about 250 USD to several thousand per night.
Guesthouses from about 50 USD with dive centres on the doorstep and cheap speedboat access, but local law applies — no alcohol on the island and covered shoulders away from designated bikini beaches.
A practical airport-adjacent base for the night before an early flight or between a liveaboard and a resort stay.

todo.title: Sandbank picnic
todo.note: Boats drop you on a bare white sandbank in the lagoon for a few hours of swimming and a packed lunch. Most guesthouses and resorts run the trip for roughly 30-70 USD per person.

todo.title: Malé old town on foot
todo.note: The capital packs the 17th-century coral-stone Friday Mosque, the fish market and the produce market into about 2 square kilometres. Two to three hours covers it between transfers.

todo.title: Local-island day trip
todo.note: Visit an inhabited island such as Maafushi, Gulhi or Guraidhoo to see schools, cafés and boatyards rather than resort lawns. Public ferries make it a 2-4 USD outing from Malé.

todo.title: Night fishing
todo.note: Handline fishing from a dhoni at sunset, Maldivian style, with the catch grilled afterwards. Runs about 2-3 hours and is offered on nearly every island.

todo.title: Sunset dolphin cruise
todo.note: Spinner dolphins move along atoll channels in the late afternoon and pods of 50+ are common. Cruises last around 2 hours.

todo.title: Bioluminescent beach walk
todo.note: On dark nights, plankton can light the tideline blue, most reliably around new moon in the wetter months. Ask locally which beach has had recent displays rather than fixing on one island.

todo.title: Surf a North Malé Atoll break
todo.note: The atoll's east side holds reliable reef breaks from March to October, with boat access from Malé, Hulhumalé and nearby islands. Lessons and board hire suit the smaller days.

todo.title: Spa afternoon
todo.note: Overwater treatment rooms with glass floors are a resort staple, and local islands have simpler, cheaper spas. A sensible surface-interval filler before a flight.

events.name: Ramadan
events.when: One lunar month, moving about 11 days earlier each year (Feb-Mar in 2026-2027); exact dates depend on moon sighting
events.note: Cafés and shops on Malé and local islands close in daylight hours and excursion schedules shrink; resort islands run normally.

events.name: Eid al-Fitr
events.when: Directly after Ramadan; a moving date confirmed by moon sighting
events.note: Multi-day public holiday with feasts and games on local islands, but domestic transport books out with Maldivians travelling home.

events.name: Independence Day
events.when: 26 July each year, with festivities starting the evening of the 25th
events.note: Parades, stadium shows and fireworks in Malé; offices close but resorts and transfers are unaffected.

Maldives.topside_summary: Above water the Maldives is 1,200 low coral islands where the choice is binary: sealed-off resort comfort or the small mosques, cafés and boatyards of local-island life. Distances are all by boat or seaplane, so most non-diving time happens within walking range of your island. Days fill with sandbanks, dolphin channels and handline fishing rather than sights in the conventional sense.

airports.name: Cairns Airport
airports.note: Main gateway for the northern and central reef; 7 km from the city centre

airports.name: Hamilton Island
